title: Bûche de Noël
subtitle: Chocolate Swiss roll filled and covered in chocolate buttercream, shaped as a yule log.
excerpt: Bûche de Noël is a French Christmas cake in the shape of a log. It is a thin chocolate sponge rolled around chocolate buttercream, then covered in more buttercream and scored with a fork to look like bark. The sponge bakes in 12 minutes and the whole thing assembles in under an hour.
sourceNotes: The bûche de Noël has been a French Christmas tradition since at least the late nineteenth century, replacing the actual Yule log that was once burned in the hearth on Christmas Eve. The cake version is attributed to various Parisian pastry chefs of the 1870s to 1890s. The format of a rolled sponge (genoise or biscuit roulé) filled and coated with ganache or buttercream, then decorated with a bark pattern, has remained largely unchanged. In Britain it was popularised in food media from the 1970s and is now a common alternative to Christmas cake.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled rolled sponge, still wrapped in paper. Defrost at room temperature, then fill and decorate.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The assembled bûche keeps in the fridge for up to three days, covered loosely. Remove 30 minutes before serving.

recipeTools.notes: Swiss roll tin, approximately 33 x 23 cm, lined with baking paper.

content.text: The sponge for a bûche de Noël is a simple whisked genoise with cocoa added. It bakes in about 12 minutes, then gets rolled up in baking paper while still warm, which sets the shape without breaking it. Once cool, you unroll it, spread on the buttercream, and roll it back up.

Decorating is quick: spread the remaining buttercream over the outside, then drag a fork along it to create a bark effect. A dusting of icing sugar gives the impression of snow. There is no need for marzipan mushrooms or other elaborate additions unless you want them.
Heat the oven to 200°C fan (220°C conventional). Line a Swiss roll tin (approximately 33 x 23 cm) with baking paper, leaving some paper overhanging the long sides.
Whisk {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} together with an electric mixer for 5 to 6 minutes. The mixture should be very pale, thick, and hold a ribbon trail from the whisk.
Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{baking-powder}} over the egg mixture. Fold gently with a large metal spoon until just combined, keeping as much air as possible.
Pour into the prepared tin and spread to the corners. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the surface springs back when pressed lightly.

While the sponge bakes, lay a fresh sheet of baking paper on the worktop and dust it with icing sugar. Turn the hot sponge immediately onto the dusted paper. Peel off the lining paper. Starting from one short end, roll the sponge up inside the fresh paper. Leave to cool completely, seam side down.

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} until very pale, about 2 minutes. Sift in {{icing-sugar}} and {{cocoa-powder}} in three additions, beating between each. Add {{whole-milk}} and beat for 3 minutes until smooth and light.

Unroll the cooled sponge gently. Spread about a third of the buttercream over the inside, leaving a 2 cm border at the far short end. Re-roll tightly using the paper to help. Transfer to a serving board, seam side down.
Use a sharp knife to cut a short diagonal slice off one end. Place it against the side of the log to create a branch. Spread the remaining buttercream all over the outside of the log and the branch.
Drag a fork along the buttercream in long strokes to create a bark pattern. Dust lightly with icing sugar to serve.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: The sponge is the one element that needs care; everything else is forgiving.

items.cause: Left to cool before rolling, or overbaked.
items.fix: Roll while still warm from the oven. A slightly cracked sponge can be disguised with buttercream; a broken one cannot be recovered. 12 minutes at the correct temperature is enough.
Beat for longer. If still too stiff, add the milk a teaspoon at a time and beat again.
